serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Banrasi Sheekh Kebab consists of 2 skewered kebab sticks weighing approximately 100 g, as commonly served as a starter or snack portion in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Banrasi Sheekh Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Banrasi Sheekh Kebab (cr_id G00010961). All per-100g macros, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol, sugar, and dietary fibre are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable vegetarian kebab preparations, as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and legume kebab
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Banrasi Sheekh Kebab. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed vegetable and chickpea-based preparations (GI range 28–45). Carbohydrate content is 5.24 g/100g; GL per 100 g serving = (35 × 5.24) / 100 = 1.83, rounded to 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Banrasi Sheekh Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100 g; if consumed, portion should not exceed 50 g and must be offset by a low-sodium meal for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) and low GI (~35) are favourable, but high fat content (26.4 g/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Banrasi Sheekh Kebab has a low glycaemic impact due to minimal carbohydrate and low GI; however, the high total fat content (26.4 g/100 g) warrants moderation, and it should not be paired with high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100 g, combined with very high sodium (1080 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (85 mg/100 g), presents a compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Banrasi Sheekh Kebab as an occasional food only; the combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and elevated cholesterol per 100 g makes regular consumption inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; cream and dairy-based binding agents may contribute pro-inflammatory fatty acids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Banrasi Sheekh Kebab due to high saturated fat content; request preparation with reduced cream and choose a chicken-based variant if available to lower arachidonic acid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banrasi Sheekh Kebab is a vegetarian Indian kebab originating from the Varanasi (Banaras) region of Uttar Pradesh, India. Unlike traditional meat-based seekh kebabs, this version is made from a spiced mixture of mixed vegetables, paneer (Indian cottage cheese), and chickpea flour (besan), shaped onto skewers and grilled or pan-fried until golden. The kebabs are seasoned with a characteristic North Indian spice blend including cumin, coriander, garam masala, green chilli, ginger, and garlic, and are often enriched with cream or ghee to achieve a soft, moist interior with a lightly charred exterior. The texture is firm yet yielding, with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a starter or snack in Indian restaurants, typically accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onion rings. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat, and only 5.24 g carbohydrate, making it low in glycaemic impact (estimated GI ~35) but high in sodium (1080 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g). It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans due to dairy content. Clinically, the very high sodium content makes it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, and the high saturated fat content warrants caution for those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis. The low carbohydrate load is favourable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, provided portion size is controlled and high-GI accompaniments are avoided.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol, sugar, and dietary fibre are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010961) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN comparable vegetarian kebab data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. All other fields are derived from these approved sources.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is accepted as a confirmed reported value from the approved source, not a default assumption. 2. saturated_fat_g (9.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from ICMR-NIN data for comparable vegetarian kebab preparations, as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this item. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish, and no equivalent ICMR-NIN or USDA entry provides these values for this specific preparation. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not available in HPB Foodo for this dish; no equivalent approved source entry covers these values for Banrasi Sheekh Kebab specifically. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable and chickpea-based preparations; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in any approved source. 6. The input description states 'Veg Kebab Sticks' confirming the vegetarian nature of this dish despite its classification under 'Meat and alternatives' in the HPB source taxonomy. 7. Cholesterol value of 85 mg/100 g is consistent with dairy (paneer, cream) content in a vegetarian preparation. 8. Ontology: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as no exact term exists for vegetarian sheekh kebab in either FoodOn or FoodEx2; the kebab parent class is the nearest defensible ancestor in both systems.
